seo.title: Ranong Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Everything about Ranong Province: Koh Phayam, Raksawarin Hot Springs, Laem Son National Park, and the Myanmar border crossing. Area: 3,298 km², population: 192,000. Plan your visit.

overview: Ranong is Thailand's smallest and wettest province, tucked into a narrow strip of the Andaman coast on the Kra Isthmus where Thailand tapers toward the Malay Peninsula. It receives more annual rainfall than any other province — around 5,000 mm per year — producing lush, year-round jungle, pristine rivers, and a strikingly green landscape that draws visitors seeking unspoiled nature. The province is best known as the gateway to Koh Phayam, a laid-back island with no cars and the finest cashew orchards in Thailand, and as the departure point for the remote, car-free Koh Chang (distinct from the better-known Koh Chang in Trat). Ranong town sits on the Kraburi River estuary, directly opposite the Myanmar town of Kawthaung (Victoria Point), making it a key land border crossing. The province's tin mining history, Chinese-Thai heritage, and natural hot springs give Ranong a character unlike anywhere else on the Andaman coast.
geography: Ranong covers 3,298 km² on the narrow Kra Isthmus, bounded by the Andaman Sea to the west and mountain ranges forming the Myanmar border to the north and east. The terrain is steeply forested with elevations up to 1,494 m at Khao Phung. The Kraburi River, which also marks the international border, drains into the estuary at Ranong town. Laem Son National Park stretches along the southern coast and protects the longest tract of intact Andaman mangroves in Thailand (about 315 km²), as well as 40 km of undeveloped beaches and a chain of offshore islands. Koh Phayam and Koh Chang lie 30–45 km offshore. The province receives more annual precipitation than any other in Thailand, with rainfall recorded at over 5,000 mm per year, supporting biodiversity-rich lowland rainforest.
highlights: Koh Phayam — a car-free island 35 km offshore with Ao Yai and Ao Khao Kwai beaches, cashew orchards, water buffalo, and the most relaxed atmosphere on the Andaman coast
Raksawarin Hot Springs — natural mineral hot springs in the middle of Ranong town, reaching 65°C at the source; a public park with foot baths open daily and free to enter
Laem Son National Park — protects Thailand's largest contiguous Andaman mangrove forest, 40 km of pristine coast, and 40 uninhabited islands accessible by longtail boat
Myanmar border crossing at Kawthaung — a 15-minute longtail ride across the Kraburi River estuary to Kawthaung (Victoria Point), Myanmar; popular for visa runs and a fascinating cross-border glimpse
Koh Chang (Ranong) — a remote, low-key island accessible only by boat, with simple bungalows, snorkeling, and no roads; not to be confused with the busier Koh Chang in Trat
Cashew nut capital — Ranong grows and processes more cashew nuts than any other province in Thailand; roadside stalls sell fresh-roasted cashews year-round at source prices
Tin mining heritage — the province's Chinese-Thai community, established during the 19th-century tin boom, is preserved in Ranong town's Sino-Portuguese shophouse architecture and Chinese shrines
Ngao Waterfall — a tiered cascade inside a protected forest reserve 15 km from town, surrounded by the province's signature year-round greenery

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Dec–Apr: The driest and sunniest months, though Ranong never experiences a true dry season like the Gulf coast. Seas are calm around Koh Phayam and boat services run reliably. Temperatures average 28–33°C. December–February brings the most comfortable conditions.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Nov and May: Transition months with a mix of sun and showers. Koh Phayam bungalows offer lower rates. The rainforest and waterfalls look spectacular after early rains. Fewer visitors than the peak period.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: The southwest monsoon arrives with intensity — Ranong is Thailand's wettest province and receives the bulk of its 5,000 mm annual rainfall in these months. Koh Phayam boat services can be suspended for days at a time. The jungle is extraordinarily green and alive. Some bungalows close entirely July–September. Hotel rates drop 40–60%.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit December to April for the best island weather and reliable boat access to Koh Phayam. If you are drawn to the raw, green intensity of the tropics at their wettest, October–November offers spectacular scenery and near-solitude. Avoid July–September for island travel.

topAttractions.name: Koh Phayam
topAttractions.description: Ranong's most celebrated island — a 34 km² car-free sanctuary reached by a 90-minute passenger ferry or 45-minute speedboat from Ranong pier. The island has no private cars or motorbikes (only ATVs on unpaved tracks), water buffalo roam freely through cashew and rubber plantations, and the pace of life is distinctly unhurried. The two main beaches, Ao Yai (Long Beach) on the southwest and Ao Khao Kwai (Buffalo Bay) on the northwest, offer fine sand and clean water. Snorkeling, kayaking, and sunset boat trips are the main activities. An established community of long-stay backpackers, yoga retreats, and European expats gives the island a relaxed, alternative character. Bungalows range from rustic fan huts (300 THB/night) to comfortable beach resorts (1,500–2,500 THB).

topAttractions.name: Raksawarin Hot Springs (Nam Phu Ron Raksawarin)
topAttractions.description: Natural mineral hot springs located within a public park just 1 km from Ranong town centre — an unusual attraction for a coastal province. The springs emerge at up to 65°C and are channelled into bathing pools. The public foot-bath pools (temperature about 40–42°C) are free; private mineral bath rooms are available for rent at the adjacent resort. The surrounding park is shaded and pleasant. A revered natural landmark tied to the town's identity — the hot springs appear on Ranong's provincial seal. Open daily from 7 AM.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free (public foot baths); private mineral baths 150–300 THB/person

topAttractions.name: Laem Son National Park
topAttractions.description: A large national park covering 315 km² of land and sea on Ranong's southern coast, protecting the longest unbroken stretch of Andaman mangroves in Thailand alongside 40 km of pristine beaches and 40 offshore islands. Hat Bang Ben beach, the park's most accessible shore, is a quiet arc of sand backed by casuarina trees with basic bungalows and a park canteen. Longtail boats available at Hat Bang Ben will ferry visitors to uninhabited islands — Koh Kam, Koh Khing, and Koh Ra — for snorkeling over undisturbed coral. The mangrove boardwalk at the park headquarters offers an excellent 30-minute walk over the root systems at high tide.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 300 THB foreigners / 30 THB Thais (adults); 150 THB / 15 THB (children)

topAttractions.name: Kawthaung Border Crossing (Myanmar / Victoria Point)
topAttractions.description: A short but memorable 15-minute longtail boat ride across the Kraburi River estuary takes visitors from Ranong's Saphan Pla pier to Kawthaung — the southernmost town in Myanmar, historically known as Victoria Point. The crossing is most commonly made as a visa run (to reset a Thai visa), but Kawthaung itself is worth exploring: the town has a lively market selling Burmese lacquerware, jade, and street food, a large Buddha image on the hillside, and a genuinely different atmosphere from Thailand. Passports required; check current entry requirements as the political situation in Myanmar may affect border access.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Longtail boat 60–100 THB return; Myanmar day pass USD 10 (approximate)

topAttractions.name: Ngao Waterfall (Nam Tok Ngao)
topAttractions.description: A multi-tiered waterfall set inside the Ngao Forest Reserve about 15 km south of Ranong town off Highway 4. The waterfall tumbles through five distinct tiers surrounded by dense lowland rainforest; the second and third tiers are the most scenic and accessible via a short trail. Swimming is possible in the natural pools during lower-flow periods. Because Ranong receives the highest annual rainfall in Thailand, Ngao flows strongly year-round — even in the 'dry' season the surrounding jungle remains lush and vivid green. Entry is free and the site is rarely crowded.

topAttractions.name: Koh Chang (Ranong)
topAttractions.description: Not to be confused with the famous Koh Chang in Trat, this Koh Chang is a small, remote island accessible by longtail from Ranong's Saphan Pla pier (approximately 1 hour). The island has no roads, no cars, and very limited tourist infrastructure — a handful of simple beachside bungalows cater to travellers seeking genuine seclusion. Snorkeling around the fringing reefs, fishing, and long walks on empty beaches are the activities on offer. Koh Chang is often visited in combination with the Myanmar border crossing, as the longtail routes overlap. Best visited November–April.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free (longtail boat charter ~400–600 THB one-way from Saphan Pla)

localFood: Cashew nuts (met mamuang) — Ranong is Thailand's cashew capital; fresh-roasted cashews sold at roadside stalls throughout the province, both salted and with garlic or chilli; far cheaper and fresher than anywhere else in Thailand
Gaeng Khua Pla (southern fish curry) — intensely spiced dry curry with fresh-caught Andaman fish, dried chillies, and wild ginger; a staple at Ranong's market restaurants
Hoy Kraeng (blood cockles) — freshly steamed blood cockles from the Andaman estuaries, served with a sharp lime-chilli-garlic dipping sauce; abundant and cheap at the morning market

Khao Tom Pla (rice soup with fish) — a classic southern Thai breakfast of rice porridge cooked with fresh sea fish, ginger, and fried garlic; commonly eaten at Ranong's early-morning Chinese-Thai shophouses
Gaeng Som Tai (sour southern fish curry) — a fiery, tamarind-based fish curry with bamboo shoots or vegetables; sharper and more pungent than central Thai versions
Dim Sum (morning) — Ranong's Chinese-Thai Hokkien heritage survives in a strong dim sum breakfast culture; steamed buns, turnip cake, and har gow served at old shophouse cafes in the town centre
Plah Tod Kamin (turmeric fried fish) — whole small fish deep-fried with fresh turmeric and lemongrass crust; a southern specialty sold at beachside stalls near Saphan Pla pier
Durian from the highlands — smaller volumes than Chumphon but Ranong's hill districts grow intensely flavoured Mon Thong and Chanee durian varieties, available at the town market April–June
culture: Ranong's cultural identity has been shaped by three forces: its Chinese-Thai tin-mining community, its position as a border province facing Myanmar, and the isolation produced by extreme rainfall and difficult terrain. The town was founded in the late 18th century by a Hokkien Chinese merchant, Khaw Soo Cheang, who was granted the tin-mining concession by the King of Thailand — a legacy visible today in the Khaw family's ancestral home (now a museum), the Chinese shrines clustered along the main street, and the prevalence of Chinese-Thai cuisine. The Burmese influence is equally present: Karen, Burmese, and Moken sea-nomad communities live within the province, and the busy border trade with Kawthaung has created a bilingual commercial strip near the pier. Traditional festivals include the annual Ranong City Pillar Shrine Festival, the Chinese New Year lantern celebrations, and the Kathin royal merit-making ceremony. Local handicrafts include Moken-style woven baskets and traditional fishing equipment.
gettingThere: Ranong town is served by Ranong Airport (UNN), located 20 km south of the town centre, with daily flights on Nok Air from Bangkok Don Mueang (approx. 1 hour 20 minutes; fares from 800 THB one-way). By bus, air-conditioned coaches depart Bangkok Southern Bus Terminal (Sai Tai Mai) several times daily, taking approximately 9–10 hours (450–700 THB). Minivans from Chumphon take about 2.5 hours (150 THB) and from Surat Thani approximately 3 hours (200 THB). There is no railway station in Ranong; the nearest rail connection is at Chumphon. For Koh Phayam, passenger ferries depart Ranong's Saphan Pla pier twice daily (usually 08:00 and 13:00), taking 90 minutes (250–350 THB); speedboats operate on demand for 45 minutes (500–600 THB). The Myanmar border crossing at Kawthaung is 15 minutes by longtail from Saphan Pla pier.
gettingAround: Within Ranong town, songthaews (shared pick-up trucks) run fixed routes between the bus terminal, the town centre, and Saphan Pla pier for 30–40 THB. Motorcycle taxis cover short distances in town for 40–80 THB. Renting a motorbike (250–350 THB/day) or car (1,000–1,500 THB/day) is strongly recommended for reaching Laem Son National Park (65 km south), Ngao Waterfall (15 km south), and the Raksawarin Hot Springs (1 km from town). On Koh Phayam, the island's dirt tracks are navigated by ATV (hired for 300–400 THB/day) or on foot and bicycle. No regular public transport connects Ranong to Laem Son — a rented vehicle or shared taxi is required.
travelTips: Pack serious rain gear regardless of the season — Ranong receives more annual rainfall than any other Thai province and showers can arrive without warning even in the dry months
Book Koh Phayam ferry tickets in advance during Dec–Feb peak season; the 08:00 morning boat from Saphan Pla pier is the most reliable option with onward connections on the island
Check the current Myanmar border crossing status before planning a visa run to Kawthaung — the political situation in Myanmar periodically affects border access
The Raksawarin Hot Springs are free to enter and just 1 km from the town centre — go early morning before the temperature climbs for the best experience
Ranong cashew nuts are the freshest and cheapest you will find in Thailand; buy vacuum-packed bags at the market or roadside stalls before departing
Laem Son National Park requires a rental vehicle to reach independently — no public transport runs there from town; the park bungalows (500–1,500 THB) must be booked via the DNP website
Ranong's Andaman island season (Dec–Apr) does not align with the Gulf coast — plan accordingly if combining Koh Phayam with Gulf islands like Koh Samui or Koh Tao
Koh Chang (Ranong) bungalows are extremely simple — bring a torch, mosquito repellent, and any snacks you need, as island supplies are very limited
